title: Sorting
description: Sorting is the gateway to countless interview optimizations — master sort(), custom comparators, lambda sort, and partial_sort so you can reach for the right tool instantly.

sections.title: sort() basics
sections.explanation: **sort(begin, end)** sorts a range in ascending order using introsort (O(n log n) guaranteed). It works on vectors, arrays, and strings. Always pass `.begin()` and `.end()` iterators. Sort is in-place and not stable — use **stable_sort** if you need to preserve equal-element order.

exercise.prompt: Sort the vector {7, 3, 9, 1, 5} in ascending order and print the elements space-separated. Then sort the string "interview" alphabetically and print it.

validation.message: Numbers sorted ascending, string sorted alphabetically
Use sort() with begin() and end() iterators

exercise.hint: sort(nums.begin(), nums.end()) sorts in place. Same pattern works for strings.

sections.title: Custom comparator
sections.explanation: Pass a comparison function as the third argument to sort. The comparator takes two elements and returns true if the first should come before the second. Use **greater<int>()** for descending order, or write a custom function for complex sorting logic.

exercise.prompt: Sort the vector {4, 1, 7, 2, 9} in descending order using greater<int>(). Print the result space-separated.

validation.message: Use greater<int>() as the comparator for descending sort

exercise.hint: Pass greater<int>() as the third argument to sort for descending order.

sections.title: Lambda sort
sections.explanation: The most flexible way to customize sorting is with a **lambda comparator**. This is the standard interview pattern for sorting pairs, structs, or by computed keys. The lambda returns true if `a` should come before `b`.

exercise.prompt: Sort a vector of pairs {("banana", 2), ("apple", 5), ("cherry", 1)} by their integer value in ascending order. Print each pair as "name: value".

validation.message: Sorted by value ascending: cherry(1), banana(2), apple(5)
Use a lambda comparing .second for ascending value sort

exercise.hint: Compare a.second < b.second for ascending order by the integer value.

sections.title: partial_sort
sections.explanation: **partial_sort(begin, middle, end)** sorts only the first (middle - begin) elements, which is O(n log k) instead of O(n log n). Use it when you only need the top-K smallest elements. The rest of the range is left in unspecified order. For top-K largest, combine with a custom comparator.

exercise.prompt: Given the vector {15, 3, 8, 1, 12, 5, 9}, use partial_sort to sort only the 4 smallest elements into the front. Print those 4 elements space-separated.

validation.message: Use partial_sort with begin + 4 as the middle iterator

exercise.hint: partial_sort(begin, begin + 4, end) places the 4 smallest elements sorted at the front.
